items.title: King Charles expressed concern over Alberta separatism in meeting with Indigenous leaders: grand chief
items.summary_auto: The grand chief of the Confederacy of Treaty 6 First Nations says King Charles "expressed his concern" after hearing about a separatist push in Alberta during a face-to-face meeting with Indigenous leaders on Wednesday.

items.title: The National | 3 cargo ships hit in Strait of Hormuz
items.summary_auto: March 11, 2026 | Three cargo ships are struck with 'unknown projectiles' in the Strait of Hormuz as Iran ramps up threats of retaliation. The Liberals cheer the former NDP MP whose defection brings them closer to a majority. And, why millennials are being warned about colorectal cancer.

items.title: Trump administration launches process to replace tariffs struck down by U.S. Supreme Court
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump's administration on Wednesday launched a trade investigation into excess industrial capacity in 16 major trading partners in a move to rebuild tariff pressure after the U.S. Supreme Court tore down the centerpiece of Trump's trade policy last month.

items.title: Social media video shows drone strike on Oman oil storage facility
items.summary_auto: A video posted to social media Wednesday shows a drone strike on an oil storage facility at Oman's Port of Salalah. CBC News verified the footage by matching the facility shown to maps and other pictures of the port, by comparing the explosion to other videos showing the facility burning and by confirming the uniform worn by a worker in the video is from a company that owns one of the ships present in the port on Wednesday.

items.title: Canada advances to World Baseball Classic playoff round for the first time
items.summary_auto: Canada has advanced to the World Baseball Classic's playoff round for the first time in the international tournament's 20-year history.

items.title: U.S. must admit responsibility for deadly airstrike on Iranian school if at fault, says Republican
items.summary_auto: A Republican senator says the U.S. must not pretend Iran school strike didn't happen, and admit to the mistake if in fact the U.S. military was responsible for the attack.

items.title: Why did filling up our gas tanks get so costly, so fast?
items.summary_auto: We know one of the impacts of the U.S.-Israel war in Iran is spiking the price of oil — but why did filling up our gas tanks get so costly, so fast?

items.title: Gas prices in Canada soar as U.S.-Israel war with Iran spreads
items.summary_auto: We know one of the impacts of the U.S.-Israel war in Iran is spiking the price of oil — but why did filling up our gas tanks get so costly, so fast?

items.title: Fact check: Old video of missile attack in Israel
items.summary_auto: This video is real, but it's been posted online with false claims that it shows missile attacks in Israel on March 7. In fact, the video is from Oct. 1, 2024. Here's how we know.

items.title: Air Transat charging more for flights to Europe as jet fuel prices weigh on airlines
items.summary_auto: Air Transat is charging more for flights to Europe as the soaring cost of jet fuel — triggered by an oil crisis that has emerged from the war in the Middle East — weighs on Canadian and international airlines.

items.title: Do new restrictions on TikTok do enough to protect Canadians' data?
items.summary_auto: TikTok can keep existing in Canada, with new measures to protect Canadians’ data. But will the measures be effective?

items.title: Federal prisons to lose librarians, employment co-ordinators in budget cuts
Federal prisons to lose library technicians, employment co-ordinators in budget cuts
items.summary_auto: Correctional Service Canada plans to cut library technicians and employment co-ordinator positions in federal penitentiaries, a move critics worry could affect inmate recidivism and reintegration.

items.title: Why would a desalination plant be a military target? | About That
items.summary_auto: Andrew Chang explains how the war in the Middle East is threatening critical freshwater infrastructure in the region. Images provided by The Canadian Press, Reuters and Getty Images

items.title: Montreal-area salon to appeal decision ordering it to pay $500 to non-binary client
items.summary_auto: The owners of a Montreal-area salon announced they will be launching an appeal after Quebec's Human Rights Tribunal ordered them to pay $500 in damages to a non-binary person who complained of discrimination.

items.title: Cuts to prison libraries at federal institutions violates human rights: advocates
items.summary_auto: The Correctional Service of Canada is finalizing plans to cut all prison librarian positions at federal institutions, in a move that advocates say violates human rights and will actively harm efforts to rehabilitate prisoners.

items.title: At least 17 killed after drone strikes school in Sudan
items.summary_auto: An explosive-laden drone blamed on Sudanese paramilitaries struck a secondary school and a health-care centre in southern Sudan Wednesday, killing at least 17 people, mostly schoolgirls, a hospital official and a medical group said.

items.title: Edmonton man facing extradition in Ryan Wedding case granted bail
items.summary_auto: An Edmonton man accused of helping alleged cocaine kingpin Ryan Wedding track down an FBI informant so he could be killed has been granted bail.

items.title: N.L. overhauls procurement after report errors thought to be AI
items.summary_auto:
The Newfoundland and Labrador government has overhauled its process for awarding contracts after false citations
thought to be generated by artificial intelligence turned up in two reports, including one prepared by Deloitte Canada.

items.title: 'Hands off our grants': High school students across Ontario leave classes to protest OSAP cuts
N.S. releases list of grants that will be reinstated after budget flip-flop
items.summary_auto: When the Nova Scotia budget was tabled more than two weeks ago, it included $130 million in cuts to discretionary grants and an additional $174 million related to job cuts. About $53 million will be restored to the budget after weeks of public outcry.

items.title: It’s going to cost $8 for passenger vehicles to cross the Gordie Howe bridge when it opens
items.summary_auto: The span will be “opening this spring,” according to a statement issued by the bridge company on Wednesday.

items.title: Alberta hit with yet another measles upswing, triggering new calls for action
items.summary_auto: Alberta experiencing a resurgence of measles and doctors say Albertans need to know the situation is not under control.

items.title: Defence closes its case in sex assault trial of Frank Stronach
items.summary_auto: The defence team for Frank Stronach wrapped up its evidence on Wednesday by seeking to undermine the credibility of the initial investigation of the sexual assault allegations made against the Canadian businessman.

items.title: Russia accuses Britain of helping Ukraine in missile strike that killed 6
items.summary_auto: The Kremlin on Wednesday accused British specialists of involvement in a deadly Ukrainian strike on the Russian city of ‌Bryansk that used British Storm Shadow missiles and said it would take Britain's role "into account."

items.title: A modest day for markets, but oil prices climb even with impending release of reserves
items.summary_auto: Most U.S. stocks fell on Wednesday as the price of oil started rising again, but markets remained calm for a second day of modest moves after a wild start to the week amid the war in Iran.

items.title: Miami Heat's Bam Adebayo scores 83 points, 2nd only to Wilt Chamberlain's single-game NBA record
items.summary_auto: Bam Adebayo had a night for all time on Tuesday, with a point total second to only Wilt Chamberlain in the NBA record books. Adebayo scored 83 points, setting league marks for free throws made and attempted in a game for the Miami Heat in a 150-129 win over the Washington Wizards.

items.title: Prices for new U.S. cars have soared. Here’s 1 big reason why
items.summary_auto: The affordability problem is a risk to American carmakers as Chinese brands expand globally. Many lower and middle-income car shoppers have been relegated to the used-car lot.

items.title: Sask. woman says boyfriend removed surgical screw poking out of her head after doctor didn't believe her
items.summary_auto: Surgeons opened Stephanie Faure's skull 14 months ago to remove brain tumours, leaving her with a metal plate, screws and a scar. So when she could see a screw pushing out through her skin, she went to the hospital to get it safely removed, but says the doctor told her it was just a cyst.

items.title: 2 people die after giving plasma at for-profit Winnipeg collection centres: Health Canada
items.summary_auto: CBC News has learned two people — including a 22-year-old international student — died after giving plasma at Winnipeg collection centres that pay people for their donations.

items.title: 2nd round of polygraphs conducted in disappearance of N.S. children amid lack of answers
items.summary_auto: Nova Scotia RCMP have conducted a second round of polygraph examinations in the disappearance of Jack and Lilly Sullivan, with two of those tests focused on one of the only pieces of physical evidence in the case: a light pink blanket found hanging in a tree.

items.title: 2nd round of polygraphs conducted in disappearance of N.S. children
items.summary_auto: Nova Scotia RCMP have conducted a second round of polygraph examinations in the disappearance of Jack and Lilly Sullivan, with two of those tests focused on one of the only pieces of physical evidence in the case: a light pink blanket found hanging in a tree.

items.title: Woman who lost 35-week pregnancy settles lawsuit with Fredericton hospital
items.summary_auto: A Hanwell woman who delivered a stillborn girl at the Chalmers Hospital in 2022 has discontinued her negligence lawsuit against Horizon Health, the hospital and four doctors. Aimee Dunn's lawyer said the case was settled out of court on terms that were "tolerable" to his client.

items.title: N.L. judge criticizes 'inflammatory rhetoric' on bail reform, as he grants bail to repeat offender
items.summary_auto: A Newfoundland judge was critical of the "jail not bail" movement during a hearing on Tuesday in Harbour Grace, where a man accused of repeatedly stealing from homes in the Conception Bay North area was granted bail.

items.title: Horses 'still dying' after flights from Canada to Japan — and regulator seems unaware
items.summary_auto: A new investigation by animal rights advocates shows horses being flown for slaughter in Japan continue to suffer injuries, illness and even death, reigniting the call for Canada to ban the shipments.

items.title: How Trump's timeline for the war on Iran keeps shifting, and why that matters
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump and his secretary of war have given multiple and often contradictory descriptions of how long they expect the military operation against Iran to continue.

items.title: Mobile brain scans sound like science fiction. But they're now helping more Canadian patients
items.summary_auto: A rising number of Canadian facilities are using mobile brain scanners to help patients, by offering MRIs mid-surgery in hospital or diagnosing stroke right on the roadside. So what's the future of this game-changing technology?

items.title: Conservation groups worry Carney's new nature strategy won't come with funding
items.summary_auto: The Carney government's strategy to protect nature is expected to be released in the coming weeks — and some nature advocacy groups worry it won't come with any new funding.

items.title: Younger Canadians are being diagnosed with colorectal cancers. Advocates want to lower the screening age
items.summary_auto: People as young as 45 should be invited to provincial and territorial programs to screen for colorectal cancer, says the Canadian Cancer Society. The call comes as emerging evidence suggests younger adults are increasingly being diagnosed with the disease before they may be eligible for screening.
